original_abstract:
We introduce a novel characterization of NP called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), a natural generalization of the span programs defined by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our primary contribution is a new approach to the arithmetization of Boolean circuits that bypasses the need for traditional Probabilistic Checkable Proofs (PCPs). By leveraging the algebraic properties of QSPs, we construct highly efficient succinct non-interactive zero-knowledge (NIZK) arguments.

In the common reference string (CRS) model, our QSP-based construction yields a NIZK for Circuit-SAT consisting of only 7 group elements, achieving the shortest proof size to date. Furthermore, our scheme features quasi-linear CRS size and prover computation, making it significantly more practical than prior techniques. We also introduce Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs), a variant of our characterization that natively handles arithmetic circuits over large fields. 

Finally, we demonstrate the utility of QSPs and QAPs in the context of publicly verifiable computation. Our resulting protocols allow a client to outsource the computation of a function $F(x)$ to an untrusted server and verify the returned result in substantially less time than a local execution would require. These constructions represent the most efficient general-purpose publicly verifiable computation schemes currently known.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce a new class of NP, called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), which can be seen as a natural extension of the class of span programs, as defined by Karchmer and Wigderson. We present a new way to arithmetize Boolean circuits, which is totally PCP free. We exploit properties of QSPs, and present very efficient succinct NIZK proofs.

At SFOC, applying our QSP-based construction to the common reference string (CRS) model gives a NIZK for Circuit-SAT with a proof of only 7 group elements, the shortest to date. In addition, our protocol has quasi-linear sized CRS and proving time which is a drastic improvement over previous approaches. We also introduce the notion of Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs) which is a relaxed version of our QSP characterization and are directly applicable to arithmetic circuits over large fields.

Finally, we show the applications of QSPs and QAPs to publicly verifiable computation. Our constructions yield very efficient protocols that allow a client to delegate the computation of a function F(x) to an untrusted server and get a proof that the server computed the desired output. The proof can be verified by the client in polylog(n) time, which is greatly faster than the total computation time of the local evaluation of the function. Our protocols are the most efficient general-purpose publicly verifiable computation protocols to date.

document.input:
